title: Effect of Small Pixel Changes on MLP

content.problemStatement:
In this demonstration, we will work with two images that represent the **same object**.

To create a small difference between them, we slightly change how light or dark the image appears.  
This is done using a single line of code, but you do **not** need to learn how this works internally.

The purpose of this step is **not image editing**.  
It is only used to simulate what already happens naturally in real-world image data.

In real life:
- Lighting conditions change
- Cameras capture brightness differently
- Writing pressure varies
- Screens and sensors are not identical

Because of this, the **same object** can appear slightly different across images, even when nothing meaningful has changed.

To humans, these images still look the same.  
To a neural network, these small visual differences become **different pixel values**.

In the code, this is done using:
```python
enhancer = ImageEnhance.Brightness(img)  
img_modified = enhancer.enhance(1.2)
```
This simply creates a second version of the same image with a small numeric change.

Now, we will use code to observe:
- How pixel values change
- How these numbers are passed as input to an MLP
- How the MLP’s final prediction reacts to this small change

---

**Expected output**
```code
Original image pixel sample: [232 232 233 232 233]
Modified image pixel sample: [255 255 255 255 255]
Average pixel difference: 209.28
Maximum pixel difference: 255
Prediction for original image: [[1. 0.]]
Prediction for modified image: [[1. 0.]]
```

**Note:** Even though the pixel values changed, the final prediction stayed the same. This happens because the MLP has almost no training experience. With many training images (such as handwritten digits), the same numeric change would influence the model’s output more clearly.
